initial_posts.narrative: As a graduate student in chemical engineering at Hanyang University, I know the BK21 program's total budget for 2022 is about 410.6 billion KRW, as stated in the policy context. For Hanyang, the allocation is part of the national total, but I estimate Hanyang's share based on its number of education research groups. Hanyang has around 20-25 groups out of 577 nationally, so roughly 3.5-4.3% of the 355.151 billion KRW for groups, plus a portion of the 52.896 billion KRW for graduate innovation. That gives about 14-18 billion KRW for Hanyang, but the policy context says total funding for all universities is 410.6 billion KRW, so I'll use that as the total allocated to the program. For graduate scholarship slots, the policy says about 12,600 per year in future talent and 6,400 in innovative talent, totaling 19,000 slots nationally. For Hanyang, with about 20 groups, each group might have 10-20 students, so 200-400 slots, but the national figure is 19,000. Postdoc positions: the policy says early-career researchers get minimum 3M KRW/month, and with the group budget, each group might support 2-5 postdocs, so nationally about 1,200 (577 groups * ~2 postdocs/group). I'm estimating based on my experience: my group has 2 postdocs, and similar groups have 1-3.
evidence: The policy context states the total annual budget for BK21 in 2022 is 408,080 million KRW for education research groups plus 52,896 million KRW for graduate innovation, totaling about 410.6 billion KRW.
The policy mentions 12,600 graduate students per year in future talent and 6,400 in innovative talent, summing to 19,000 slots nationally, which I use as the graduate_scholarship_slots.
With 577 education research groups and each group typically supporting 1-3 postdocs, a conservative estimate of 2 per group yields about 1,154 postdoc positions, rounded to 1,200.

initial_posts.narrative: As the head of an education research group at Ewha Womans University, I rely on the policy context which states the total BK21 budget for 2022 is 410.597 billion KRW. This includes 355.151 billion for education research groups, 52.896 billion for graduate innovation, and 2.55 billion for evaluation. The graduate scholarship slots are clearly stated as about 12,600 for future talent and 6,400 for innovative talent, totaling 19,000 nationally. For postdoc positions, I estimate based on the minimum salary of 3M KRW/month and typical group budgets. With 577 groups and an average of about 2 postdocs per group (as seen in my own group and others), that gives roughly 1,200 positions nationally. I disagree with the graduate student's estimate of Hanyang-specific funding; the policy context provides national totals, not university-specific breakdowns, so I use the national figures as given.
evidence: The policy context explicitly states the total budget is 410,597 million KRW for 2022, including all components.
The policy states about 12,600 graduate students per year in future talent and 6,400 in innovative talent, totaling 19,000 slots.
With 577 education research groups and typical postdoc numbers of 1-3 per group, 1,200 postdoc positions is a reasonable national estimate based on the minimum salary requirement.

initial_posts.narrative: As a graduate student in chemical engineering at Hanyang University, I see the activities phase as the operational engine of the BK21 program. The total funds disbursed should closely match the allocated budget of 408,080 million KRW for education research groups, as the 70% March and 30% September schedule is standard and efficiently executed by the NRF. Evaluation panels are convened for annual, mid-term, and compliance purposes, but in 2022, only the annual evaluation and compliance checks are active (mid-term is 2023), so I estimate 3 formal panel meetings (annual evaluation, graduate innovation evaluation, and one compliance check meeting). Consulting sessions are offered to groups that request them; the policy states 280 groups received consulting out of 578, so I use that figure. Compliance checks target the 205 innovative talent training groups, as per the policy, so I estimate 205 checks executed. Budget adjustments are handled by the Project Oversight Committee based on evaluation outcomes, but in 2022, the first-year adjustments are minimal, so I don't factor significant reallocations into disbursed amounts.
evidence: The policy states the budget payment schedule is 70% in March and 30% in September, totaling 408,080 million KRW for education research groups, which I expect to be fully disbursed in 2022.

The policy mentions annual evaluation, mid-term evaluation (2023), and compliance checks, so in 2022, I estimate 3 formal evaluation panel meetings: annual evaluation, graduate innovation evaluation, and one compliance check meeting.
The policy explicitly states that consulting was conducted for 280 education research groups out of 578 that wished to receive consulting, so I use that number.
The policy says 205 education research groups are subject to compliance checks on implementation, so I estimate 205 compliance checks executed in 2022.

initial_posts.narrative: As a graduate student in chemical engineering at Hanyang University, I see the outputs phase as the direct result of the funding and activities phases. With 19,000 graduate scholarship slots and 1,200 postdoc positions funded nationally, the program is designed to support a large cohort of researchers. Based on my own experience and that of my peers, each graduate student typically produces 2-3 publications per year, and postdocs produce 3-5. I conservatively estimate 3 publications per supported researcher (graduate or postdoc) on average, yielding about 57,000 publications (19,000+1,200=20,200 researchers × 2.8 ≈ 57,000). For international conference presentations, about 20% of these publications are presented at international conferences, so roughly 11,400 presentations. This aligns with the program's emphasis on internationalization and research output. The high compliance in self-evaluation reporting (575 out of 578 groups) suggests that reporting is reliable, so these output numbers are likely accurate.

I have not seen other participants' posts yet, so my estimates are independent.
